questions.prompt: The voltage gain of a CE amplifier with fully bypassed RE is:
questions.explanation: With RE=0 (bypassed): Av = −hfe·RC/hie. The negative sign indicates 180° phase inversion, characteristic of CE.

questions.prompt: Adding un-bypassed RE to a CE amplifier does what to the gain?
questions.explanation: With RE: Av = −hfe·RC/(hie + hfe·RE). Since hfe·RE >> hie, gain ≈ −RC/RE which is smaller but very stable.

questions.prompt: The input impedance of a CE amplifier (looking into the base) with RE is:
questions.explanation: RE appears (1+hfe) times larger when seen from the base. This 'impedance reflection' is a fundamental BJT property.
CC is a voltage follower: vo ≈ vin. It provides current gain and impedance transformation but not voltage gain.

questions.prompt: The CC configuration is used primarily for:
questions.explanation: CC has very high Rin and very low Rout, making it ideal as a buffer between a high-impedance source and a low-impedance load.

questions.prompt: The Common-Base input impedance is approximately:
questions.explanation: CB input R = hie/(1+hfe) ≈ 1000/101 ≈ 10 Ω. Very low — good for current-mode signals.

questions.prompt: The effect of RL on voltage gain is:
options: RL decreases gain (appears in parallel with RC)
questions.correctAnswer: RL decreases gain (appears in parallel with RC)
questions.explanation: RL || RC is smaller than RC alone, so the effective load (and hence gain) decreases when RL is connected.

questions.prompt: The overall gain Avs (from vs to vo) includes:
questions.explanation: Avs = Av × [Rin/(Rin+Rs)] × [RL/(RL+Rout)]. Both source and load resistances reduce the overall gain.
CE inverts: the negative sign in Av = −hfe·RC/(hie+hfe·RE). When input goes up, output goes down.

questions.prompt: The output impedance Rout of a CE amplifier (simplified) is:
questions.explanation: With hoe=0 (simplified model), Rout = RC. With hoe: Rout = RC || (1/hoe).
